question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a crafted blockTimestamp value (e.g., Long.MAX_VALUE or a future nanosecond timestamp) to findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to cause the underlying SQL query (consensus_timestamp <= ?3) to return the absolute latest contract_state_change record regardless of the intended historical block, effectively rewriting the perceived state history at any past block to reflect the most recent value?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass blockTimestamp=0 to findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to force the query (consensus_timestamp <= 0) to return empty for every slot, making all historical contract state appear as if it never existed at any block, thereby reorganizing the visible transaction history to show a blank state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a blockTimestamp exactly equal to a known consensus_timestamp of a targeted transaction to findStorageByBlockTimestamp(), causing the coalesce(value_written, value_read) logic to return value_read instead of value_written when value_written is null, making the historical record appear as if a write never occurred at that block?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a slotKeyByteArray with incorrect padding or length (e.g., fewer than 32 bytes) to findStorageByBlockTimestamp() such that the SQL slot = ?2 comparison silently matches a different slot in contract_state_change, causing the historical state of a different storage slot to be returned and misrepresenting the transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass a negative blockTimestamp (e.g., -1L) to findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to exploit the consensus_timestamp <= ?3 comparison with a signed long, potentially causing unexpected query behavior that returns no records or incorrect records, reorganizing the apparent history of the contract slot?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly call findStorageByBlockTimestamp() with the same contractId and slotKeyByteArray but incrementally varying blockTimestamp values to enumerate the full write history of a contract storage slot, reconstructing a complete transaction timeline that the contract author intended to keep private?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the coalesce(value_written, value_read) fallback in the underlying SQL query of findStorageByBlockTimestamp() by targeting a block where value_written is null, causing the returned value to reflect value_read (a pre-write snapshot), making the historical record appear as if a state-changing transaction at that block never wrote any value?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a blockTimestamp that is exactly one nanosecond before a known state-changing transaction's consensus_timestamp to findStorageByBlockTimestamp(), causing the query to skip that transaction and return the prior state, effectively making the system present a reorganized view of history where that transaction appears not to have occurred?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass an all-zero slotKeyByteArray (32 zero bytes) to findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to probe whether slot 0 of any contract has historical state changes, and if so, use the returned consensus_timestamp ordering to infer the transaction history sequence of that contract?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any rate limiting or access control on findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to perform a high-volume timestamp sweep across all slots of a target contract, reconstructing the full ordered transaction history and identifying the exact consensus_timestamp of every state change?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the EMPTY_VALUE sentinel check (cachedValue != EMPTY_VALUE) in findStorage() by causing a race condition where a slot is inserted into contractSlotsCache with EMPTY_VALUE and then the cache is read before the batch DB query completes, causing findStorage() to fall through to findStorageBatch() repeatedly and serve inconsistent historical state views to concurrent callers?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorage()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger cache poisoning in findStorage() by flooding the contractStateCache with requests for non-existent slot keys, causing legitimate slot keys to be evicted, and then exploiting the isKeyEvictedFromCache fallback path in findStorageBatch() to force a direct DB query that may return a different (older) value than what was previously cached, creating a history inconsistency?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorageBatch() reads cachedSlotKeys from contractSlotsCache.getNativeCache().asMap().keySet() without synchronization, causing a concurrent modification scenario where a slot key added by another thread is included in the batch query, and the returned value for that key is cached under the requesting user's contractId, cross-contaminating historical state between contracts?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ByteBuffer.wrap(key) call in findStorageBatch() which shares the underlying byte array, by mutating the original key array after the ByteBuffer is created but before the batch query executes, causing the wrong slot key to be queried and a different contract slot's historical value to be cached under the original key?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the isKeyEvictedFromCache flag in findStorageBatch() to remain true by timing their request to arrive exactly when the Caffeine cache evicts the target slot key, forcing a fallback to contractStateRepository.findStorage() which bypasses the batch result and returns a potentially stale or different value, creating a discrepancy between what the cache and DB report as the current state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorageBatch() calls contractSlotsCache.putIfAbsent(wrappedKey, EMPTY_VALUE) before reading cachedSlotKeys, and then iterates over the live key set, to inject a large number of slot keys into the per-contract cache, causing the batch query to include thousands of irrelevant slots, and the resulting contractStateCache to be populated with values from a different point in time than the caller intended?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shared contractSlotsCache (keyed by contractId) in findStorageBatch() by making concurrent requests for the same contractId with different slot keys, causing the batch query to include slot keys from multiple concurrent requests, and the resulting cache population to associate values from one request's DB result with slot keys from another request, reorganizing which historical value is attributed to which slot?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the two-phase cache check in findStorage() (first checking contractStateCache, then calling findStorageBatch()) by timing a request to arrive after a cache expiry but before a DB refresh, causing the service to return Optional.empty() for a slot that has a valid historical value, making the contract's state appear as if it was never written?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorage() returns Optional.of(cachedValue) when cachedValue != EMPTY_VALUE without verifying the cached value is still consistent with the DB, causing a stale cached value to be returned long after the underlying contract_state has been updated, making historical queries appear to show an older transaction ordering?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass a blockTimestamp equal to Long.MAX_VALUE (9223372036854775807) to findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to always retrieve the most recent state regardless of the requested block, and then compare results with a lower timestamp to infer whether a state-changing transaction occurred between two timestamps, reconstructing the transaction ordering?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of validation on the entityId parameter in findStorageByBlockTimestamp() by passing an EntityId that does not correspond to any deployed contract, causing the query to return empty and making it appear as if a contract's entire transaction history was erased at a given block?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the order by consensus_timestamp desc limit 1 in the underlying SQL of findStorageByBlockTimestamp() by identifying a blockTimestamp that falls between two consecutive state changes, causing the query to return the earlier state and making the system present a view of history where the later transaction appears not to have happened yet?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user use binary search over blockTimestamp values in findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to precisely identify the consensus_timestamp of every state-changing transaction for a target contract slot, effectively reconstructing the full transaction history ordering without any special permissions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the coalesce(value_written, value_read) logic in findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to distinguish between transactions that wrote a value versus those that only read a value at a given consensus_timestamp, allowing them to reconstruct which transactions were writes versus reads and reorganize the perceived write history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorageBatch() uses contractSlotsCache.getNativeCache().asMap().keySet() (a live view of the Caffeine cache) to enumerate all slot keys currently cached for a target contract, effectively discovering which storage slots have been recently accessed and inferring the transaction activity pattern of that contract?
